target.title: feat(plugins): add model failover and terminal failure hooks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds two plugin observability hooks for model failure handling:

- `model_failover`: observes embedded-runner failover decisions, including failed source refs, selected target refs, stage, decision, reason metadata, recoverability, timeout/abort/status flags, and fallback availability.
- `model_failure_terminal`: observes terminal before-reply model failures after all configured attempts are exhausted.

This PR does not change fallback policy, provider retry behavior, context re-injection behavior, command/tool execution, network access, or default user-facing failure copy.

## Current implementation

- Emits `model_failover` from the embedded-runner failover decision path.
- Emits `model_failure_terminal` after resolving the actual delivered failure reply, so `finalMessage` matches user-visible failure copy.
- Propagates the selected fallback candidate through the normal gateway/auto-reply path before running the embedded agent.
- Registers both hook names and payload types in the Plugin SDK surface and refreshes the SDK API baseline hash.
- Bounds both failure-path void hooks with default 30s timeout budgets and fail-open behavior.

## Security / compatibility

- New public Plugin SDK hook contract: `model_failover`, `model_failure_terminal`.
- `model_failure_terminal` is conversation-access gated for non-bundled plugins because it can expose delivered failure copy.
- Attempt summaries expose provider/model/reason metadata, not raw provider error blobs.
- Maintainers still need to accept the public hook contract and terminal-failure data boundary before release.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A real embedded OpenClaw model run should emit `model_failover` when the primary model fails and the runtime selects the configured fallback target. A separate exhausted-model run should emit `model_failure_terminal` after all configured model attempts fail, with `finalMessage` matching the user-visible failure copy and `attempts` summarizing provider/model/reason metadata.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS Darwin 25.3.0 arm64, Node `v25.6.0`, pnpm `11.2.2`, PR head `87781dfba46b73ee90ca3e3babf33924b1c9d68c`.
- **Exact setup**: The proof script created a temporary workspace, temporary state dir, and a real workspace plugin with `openclaw.plugin.json`. OpenClaw loaded that plugin through `loadOpenClawPlugins`; the plugin registered `api.on("model_failover", ...)` and `api.on("model_failure_terminal", ...)` and wrote hook payloads to a redacted JSONL artifact. The model providers were deterministic local OpenAI-compatible Responses endpoints. Scenario 1 returned HTTP 429 from the primary endpoint and a successful SSE canary from fallback. Scenario 2 returned HTTP 429 from both primary and fallback to exhaust all configured model attempts. No external provider token was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
OPENCLAW_MODEL_FAILOVER_PROOF_HEAD=$(git rev-parse --short=12 HEAD) node --import tsx .artifacts/model-failover-real-proof.mjs
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

The second source-built run hit both terminal primary and terminal fallback endpoints, exhausted all configured attempts, emitted `model_failure_terminal`, and the hook `finalMessage` matched the delivered final reply while `attempts` contained both failed provider/model summaries with `rate_limit` reasons.
- **What was not tested**: The provider endpoints are deterministic local redacted endpoints rather than live third-party provider outages; no private tokens, phone numbers, IP addresses, or non-public external endpoints are included.

target.title: feat(sessions): add adaptive reset mode with daily AND idle semantics
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Session reset currently supports only `daily` and `idle`, and when both are configured the behavior is OR-based, which can reset an active conversation as soon as the daily boundary passes.
- Why it matters: Some users want the daily rollover guard without interrupting active conversations, meaning reset should happen only after the boundary has passed and the session has gone idle.
- What changed: Added `mode: "adaptive"` as a narrow reset mode that requires both the daily boundary and the idle window before a session becomes stale, and updated the schema/help/session wiring to match.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add new reset fields, change the default reset mode, or alter existing `daily` and `idle` semantics.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #43738
- Related #44489
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `session.reset.mode` accepts a new value: `"adaptive"`.
- `adaptive` resets a session only when both conditions are met:
  - the daily reset boundary has passed
  - the configured idle window has expired
- Existing `daily` and `idle` behavior remains unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[daily stale] OR [idle stale] -> reset

After:
mode=daily/idle -> existing behavior unchanged
mode=adaptive -> [daily stale] AND [idle stale] -> reset
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): session reset policy with `atHour` and `idleMinutes`

### Steps

1. Configure session reset mode as `adaptive` with `atHour` and `idleMinutes`.
2. Evaluate freshness when only the daily boundary is crossed.
3. Evaluate freshness when only the idle window expires.
4. Evaluate freshness when both are true.

### Expected

- Daily-only stale: session remains fresh.
- Idle-only stale: session remains fresh.
- Both stale: session resets.
- Existing `daily` and `idle` modes behave exactly as before.

### Actual

- Added adaptive-mode policy tests plus schema/session behavior coverage.
- Updated schema/help/generated config surfaces and session handling.
- Focused Vitest suite passed.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `session.reset.mode: "adaptive"` should keep an existing session fresh when only the daily boundary or only the idle window is stale, and should roll the session only when both conditions are stale.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, running this branch's real `initSessionState` path against a temporary on-disk session store under `~/.openclaw/state/tmp`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a one-shot runtime script from the branch with `pnpm exec tsx` that seeded three real session rows, configured `session.reset.mode: "adaptive"`, and called `initSessionState` for daily-only, idle-only, and both-stale cases.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from that real runtime path:

  ```text
  OpenClaw adaptive reset runtime proof
  tempStore=/Users/mantis/.openclaw/state/tmp/openclaw-adaptive-real-proof-hn8QYH/sessions.json
  policy=mode:adaptive atHour:8 idleMinutes:60
  daily-only stale stays fresh: isNewSession=false previous=adaptive-daily-only result=adaptive-daily-only
  idle-only stale stays fresh: isNewSession=false previous=adaptive-idle-only result=adaptive-idle-only
  daily+idle stale rolls session: isNewSession=true previous=adaptive-both-stale result=45327a3a-ef16-44a5-a950-96a8bc242f29
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The actual session runtime preserved the current session when only one adaptive condition was stale, and created a new session only when both daily and idle conditions were stale.
- **What was not tested:** Full interactive Telegram delivery flow; this proof targets the real OpenClaw session initialization/reset path that owns the adaptive rollover decision.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - adaptive policy stays fresh when only one side is stale
  - adaptive policy resets when both daily and idle conditions are stale
  - schema accepts `mode: "adaptive"`
  - session-related tests still pass with the new mode wired through
- Edge cases checked:
  - adaptive mode defaults an idle window when omitted in policy resolution
  - schema/help/generated config surfaces remain aligned
- What you did **not** verify:
  - remote GitHub CI beyond the local focused suite and `check:changed`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: AND/OR freshness logic could be implemented incorrectly and silently change reset behavior.
  - Mitigation: Added focused policy tests for both-one-neither stale combinations while preserving existing mode behavior.
- Risk: Config/schema layers could drift if `adaptive` is added in one validation surface but not another.
  - Mitigation: update type union, Zod schema, generated schema/help text, and session wiring together.
- Risk: stale-session reason/reporting paths could become misleading if adaptive mode is only partially wired.
  - Mitigation: included session-related tests and the current session/reset handling surfaces in the same change.

## Latest local validation

Current head: `b61b8af26c1e2903afec2810937d2ac3dc24c78e`

Branch history: rebuilt from current upstream into one feature commit and force-pushed after dropping stale rebase/sync churn.

Local validation on the squashed head:

- `pnpm config:docs:check` — passed
- `pnpm test src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.session-maintenance-extensions.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/session-hooks-context.test.ts` — passed
- `pnpm deadcode:unused-files` — passed
- `pnpm lint --threads=8` — passed

GitHub checks on the current head while updating this proof:

- `gh pr checks 71400 --repo openclaw/openclaw --json name,state,workflow` — 124 success, 28 skipped, 1 neutral, 0 failing, 0 pending
- `gh pr view 71400 --repo openclaw/openclaw --json mergeable,headRefOid` — head matches `b61b8af26c1e2903afec2810937d2ac3dc24c78e`; mergeability currently reports `MERGEABLE`

target.title: feat(hooks): add agent turn end hook
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a clean `agent:turn:end` internal hook event for ACP runtime turn completion. This split intentionally keeps the event as a runtime lifecycle boundary only; it does not claim transcript/session-write durability.

## Changes
- Registers and validates `agent:turn:end` hook events with `sessionKey`, `success`, `durationMs`, and optional `errorCode` context.
- Emits the event from the ACP manager after terminal runtime turn handling and awaited ACP event-delivery callbacks.
- Does not emit `agent:turn:end` for pre-runtime session initialization failures such as max-concurrency rejection.
- Documents the lifecycle-only contract and explicitly states that this event does not prove durable session writes.
- Adds focused ACP manager and internal hook tests for success, failure, ordering, and pre-runtime init-failure gating.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: proves the clean split branch emits `agent:turn:end` from an actual ACP runtime turn, while keeping save/write-durability concerns out of this PR and avoiding turn-end emission for pre-runtime init failures.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS at `/Users/mantis/Projects/openclaw-pr-turn-end-clean`, branch `feat/agent-turn-end-hook-clean`, rebased onto current `origin/main`, head `52d28708c0`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: started the source-built Gateway from this branch with a temporary config/state directory, enabled the embedded `acpx` runtime plugin, loaded a temporary internal hook through `hooks.internal.load.extraDirs`, initialized a real ACPX/Codex persistent session using a temporary copied `CODEX_HOME`, ran `AcpSessionManager.runTurn()` with the prompt `Reply with exactly TURN-END-HOOK-PROOF-OK`, then attempted a second runtime session while `acp.maxConcurrentSessions` was `1` to force a pre-runtime `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` path.
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):
```text
$ git rev-parse --short=10 HEAD
52d28708c0
$ OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 OPENCLAW_LIVE_ACP_BIND_CODEX_MODEL=gpt-5.5 \
  OPENCLAW_TURN_END_SOURCE_CODEX_HOME=/Users/mantis/.codex \
  OPENCLAW_TURN_END_PROOF_HEAD=$(git rev-parse --short=10 HEAD) \
  node --import tsx .artifacts/turn-end-proof-run.mjs
{
  "ok": true,
  "head": "52d28708c0",
  "initializedBackend": "acpx",
  "successSessionKey": "agent:proof-codex:acp:turn-end-proof-f0e595eb-8a63-42f0-80aa-b6e373699f0c",
  "blockedSessionKey": "agent:proof-codex:acp:turn-end-blocked-4cdae395-c82e-4e0f-b466-ef6a1e0f1486",
  "successHookContext": {
    "sessionKey": "agent:proof-codex:acp:turn-end-proof-f0e595eb-8a63-42f0-80aa-b6e373699f0c",
    "success": true,
    "durationMs": 4200
  },
  "blockedError": {
    "name": "AcpRuntimeError",
    "code": "ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED",
    "message": "ACP max concurrent sessions reached (1/1)."
  },
  "hookEventCount": 1,
  "blockedHookEventCount": 0,
  "proofLog": "/Users/mantis/.openclaw/state/tmp/openclaw-turn-end-proof-wuPoN6/turn-end-proof.jsonl"
}
```
- Additional focused validation:
```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 6.99s
$ pnpm check:test-types
<exit 0>
$ pnpm exec oxlint src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.turn-runner.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test-helpers.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.types.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts
<exit 0>
$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs/automation/hooks.md src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.turn-runner.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test-helpers.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.types.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
$ git diff --check origin/main...HEAD && git diff --check
<exit 0>
```
- Observed result after fix: the temporary hook captured exactly one real `agent:turn:end` event from a live ACPX/Codex turn with `success: true`; the pre-runtime max-concurrency failure returned `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` and captured zero `agent:turn:end` events for that blocked session.
- What was not tested: production gateway deployment. This was intentionally run against a temporary local Gateway/config/state using source code from this branch.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the proof uses local Codex auth copied into a temporary Codex home and a one-off local proof harness that is not committed; hook handlers are internal and only observable when an operator installs/enables a hook.

## Testing
- Real source-built ACPX/Codex turn proof described above
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

target.title: fix(mcp): skip unreadable plugin tools
target.body:
## Summary

- Hardens the plugin tools MCP bridge so unreadable or unwrappable plugin tool descriptors are skipped during handler construction instead of crashing `listTools`.
- Keeps healthy sibling plugin tools listed and callable through the MCP bridge, including the existing `before_tool_call` report-mode wrapper behavior.
- Adds focused regression coverage for throwing `name`, throwing `parameters`, and revoked proxy schema descriptors alongside a healthy sibling tool.
- Out of scope: runtime plugin registry validation and non-MCP provider/tool projection paths.

Transcript note: agent transcript finder returned candidate local sessions; no transcript excerpt is included because approval is required before adding session logs.

## Linked context

Closes: none.
Related: ongoing tool-schema fuzzing hardening workstream.
Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? Maintainer fuzzing sweep.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a malformed plugin tool descriptor no longer aborts plugin MCP handler construction or `listTools`; only the malformed descriptor is omitted while valid sibling tools remain exposed and callable.
Real environment tested: local macOS linked gwt worktree rebased onto `origin/main` at `4c32553875b86c27439305c8cc163a03044ecd41`; `origin/main` later advanced to `0a351cdf7f42f03d82643774274b0a26aee66f62` with no changes under `src/mcp/plugin-tools-handlers.ts` or `src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts src/mcp/plugin-tools-handlers.cancel.test.ts src/mcp/openclaw-tools-serve.test.ts --reporter=dot --pool=forks --maxWorkers=1 --fileParallelism=false --teardownTimeout=1000`; `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/mcp/plugin-tools-handlers.ts src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/mcp/plugin-tools-handlers.ts src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts`; `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD -- src/mcp/plugin-tools-handlers.ts src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts`; `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`; `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --idle-timeout 5m --ttl 20m --timing-json --shell -- "pwd >/dev/null"`.
Evidence after fix: focused Vitest passed 3 files / 10 tests across 2 shards; oxfmt passed; oxlint passed; diff whitespace check passed; final branch autoreview returned `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported` with `overall: patch is correct (0.84)`.
Observed result after fix: the regression builds MCP handlers from bad descriptors plus `healthy_tool`; `listTools` returns only `healthy_tool`, preserves its input schema, and `callTool` executes it successfully.
What was not tested: live stdio MCP client roundtrip beyond the existing in-process cancellation/list/call coverage; broad changed gate was unavailable.
Proof limitations or environment constraints: Blacksmith Testbox is unavailable because `blacksmith testbox list --all` reports `not authenticated`; AWS Crabbox probe is currently blocked by coordinator 401 on run-history/lease creation, so no fresh remote changed gate was available for this PR.
Before evidence: `createPluginToolsMcpHandlers` mapped/wrapped/listed every descriptor with direct `tool.name`, `tool.description`, and `tool.parameters` reads, so one hostile plugin tool descriptor could throw before the MCP bridge listed healthy tools.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts src/mcp/plugin-tools-handlers.cancel.test.ts src/mcp/openclaw-tools-serve.test.ts --reporter=dot --pool=forks --maxWorkers=1 --fileParallelism=false --teardownTimeout=1000`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/mcp/plugin-tools-handlers.ts src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/mcp/plugin-tools-handlers.ts src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD -- src/mcp/plugin-tools-handlers.ts src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`

Remote proof attempted:
- `blacksmith testbox list --all` -> blocked: not authenticated.
- `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --idle-timeout 5m --ttl 20m --timing-json --shell -- "pwd >/dev/null"` -> blocked: coordinator 401 creating run history/lease.

Regression coverage added: `src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.test.ts` now covers unreadable plugin tool names, unreadable plugin tool parameters, revoked proxy schemas, and healthy sibling preservation in MCP listing/calling.

What failed before this fix: descriptor wrapping/listing could throw before MCP `listTools` returned, which made one bad plugin tool poison the whole plugin-tools MCP server surface.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? Yes: malformed plugin tools are omitted from the plugin-tools MCP bridge instead of crashing the bridge.
Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No.
Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No.
What is the highest-risk area? Accidentally hiding valid plugin tools while filtering malformed descriptors.
How is that risk mitigated? The filter only drops descriptors whose name, parameters, schema readability, or hook wrapping throws; existing valid listing, calling, approval-report wrapping, and cancellation coverage stayed green.

## Current review state

What is the next action? Maintainer review and CI once remote runner auth is available.
What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? GitHub CI/remote changed gate; current local runner auth blocks Testbox and Crabbox proof.
Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? Local autoreview found no accepted/actionable findings.

target.title: feat(voice-call): add Microsoft Teams voice provider (OpenClawTeamsBridge)
target.body:
## Summary

**What problem does this PR solve?**
- OpenClaw's `voice-call` extension supports Twilio, Telnyx, Plivo, and a mock provider, but has no way to handle **Microsoft Teams** calls. Teams audio cannot reach the existing webhook/media-stream plane because Teams calls are delivered through Graph Calling signalling + the `Microsoft.Skype.Bots.Media` AudioSocket, which only run on .NET/Windows.
- This adds a 5th voice-call provider, **`msteams`**, that bridges Teams call audio into the existing voice-call machinery (CallManager, realtime transcription, response generation, telephony TTS).

**Why does this matter now?**
- It unlocks real-time voice agents on Microsoft Teams using the same OpenClaw agent/STT/TTS stack already used for PSTN providers, with no changes to those providers.

**What is the intended outcome?**
- An operator can set `provider: "msteams"` and OpenClaw will accept per-call WebSocket connections from an external Windows worker ("OpenClawTeamsBridge" / AzureBot), authenticate them, and run the full inbound->STT->agent->TTS->outbound loop with barge-in  -  mirroring the streaming Twilio provider but speaking **PCM 16 kHz** instead of mu-law 8 kHz.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**
- The Windows-side worker (Graph Calling notification endpoint + `Skype.Bots.Media` AudioSocket) is a **separate component, not included in this repo**. This PR only adds the OpenClaw-side provider + WebSocket transport it connects to.
- Outbound dialing (initiating Teams calls)  -  the provider is inbound-only and throws on `initiateCall`.

**What does success look like?**
- `provider: "msteams"` binds an HMAC-authenticated WebSocket server; a live Teams call routed through the external worker produces a transcript, an agent reply, and synthesized speech back to the caller, with barge-in.

**What should reviewers focus on?**
- The WebSocket auth/transport in `msteams-media-stream.ts` (HMAC verification, replay window, payload cap, and loopback-by-default bind).
- The msteams config-validation in `config.ts`: required `port` + SecretRef-compatible `sharedSecret`, the `fromNumber` exemption, and `inboundPolicy` defaulting to "open".
- The runtime lifecycle in `runtime.ts`: the media server starts via an awaited `provider.start()` (a failed bind aborts init) and stops on teardown so the port is released.
- That the new provider reuses existing exported helpers (`generateVoiceResponse`, `CallManager.speak`, telephony TTS) rather than duplicating webhook logic.

---

## Linked context

**Which issue does this close?**

Closes # <!-- fill in if a tracking issue exists; otherwise remove -->

**Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?**

Related # <!-- e.g. prior Telnyx/Twilio realtime streaming PRs this mirrors -->

**Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?**

<!-- Note any maintainer/owner request here, or state "Community contribution." -->

---

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `voice-call` had no Microsoft Teams provider  -  `provider: "msteams"` was rejected as unsupported. This PR adds the provider, the HMAC-authenticated WebSocket transport, and the inbound->STT->agent->TTS->outbound pipeline.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw 2026.6.2 local gateway on Windows Server (Azure VM); `voice-call` provider `msteams`; agent `microsoft-foundry/gpt-5.4`; ElevenLabs realtime STT (`scribe_v2_realtime`, `pcm_16000`) + ElevenLabs TTS; an external Windows worker bridged a real inbound Microsoft Teams call.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. `pnpm build`
  2. `node openclaw.mjs config validate`  -> prints `Config valid`
  3. `node openclaw.mjs gateway run --force`
  4. Placed a real Microsoft Teams call to the bot, spoke several turns out loud, heard the bot's spoken replies, then hung up.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  (a) Gateway lifecycle log during the call (private identifiers redacted):
  ```
  MsteamsMediaStream listening port=8443 path=/voice/msteams/stream
  [voice-call] msteams realtime transcription provider: elevenlabs
  [voice-call] msteams TTS provider configured
  MsteamsMediaStream: connection open <callId>
  MsteamsProvider: session.start callId=<callId> threadId=<threadId> caller.aadId=<aadId>
  MsteamsMediaStream: connection closed <callId>
  ```
  (b) Agent session trajectory for the same call (`sessionKey: voice:<redacted>`, model `microsoft-foundry/gpt-5.4`)  -  the inbound caller audio was transcribed by ElevenLabs STT and answered by the agent; each `{"spoken":...}` reply is what was synthesized back over the bridge as TTS the caller heard:
  ```
  user  (STT): "How are you, man?"
  asst (spoken): "I'm doing well, man  -  thanks. What can I help you with?"
  user  (STT): "Tell me more about you."
  asst (spoken): "I'm your phone assistant in OpenClaw  -  calm, practical, and here to help with
                  questions, quick research, reminders, messages, and getting things done..."
  user  (STT): "Thank you. And, uh, what is your-"
  asst (spoken): "I'm an AI phone assistant running in OpenClaw. I don't have a body or a personal
                  life, but I can talk with you, look things up, help you plan..."
  user  (STT): "Mm-hmm"
  asst (spoken): "Yeah  -  kind of like a calm digital helper you can just talk to..."
  ```
- Observed result after fix: full bidirectional audio round-trip on a live Teams call  -  caller speech -> ElevenLabs STT transcript -> `gpt-5.4` agent reply -> ElevenLabs TTS played back to the caller, across multiple turns over ~70 s. The speech disfluencies ("uh", "Mm-hmm") and natural turn-taking confirm these are live ASR transcripts (not typed input); the `{"spoken":...}` payloads are exactly what the provider sends to TTS. Session lifecycle (`connection open` / `session.start` / `connection closed`) is verified in the gateway log.
- What was not tested: outbound dialing (the provider is inbound-only and out of scope); sustained barge-in and high-concurrency load have not yet been captured under real calls.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: transcript/reply text is taken from the agent session trajectory because the gateway file log does not record transcript text at info level. Private identifiers (callId, threadId, caller aadId, sessionKey) are redacted.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, `provider: "msteams"` produced `Unsupported voice-call provider: msteams`.

### After-fix proof: realtime delegation + recording-status gate (latest commits)

Captured on real inbound Microsoft Teams calls using the realtime (speech-to-speech) path (`realtime.enabled`, provider `openai`, `toolPolicy: "owner"`), with the recording gate at its default (`requireRecordingStatus: true`). Private identifiers redacted; call ids shortened.

- Recording gate refuses, then opens. With no recording signal, asking the assistant to do work was refused out loud ("recording isn't active") and the gateway logged no `recording.status`. Once the external worker reports recording active on call establish, the gateway opens the gate:
  ```
  # delegation refused (no recording signal yet)
  MsteamsProvider: realtime session.start callId=0c005180... caller.aadId=<redacted>

  # next call: worker reports recording active -> gate opens
  MsteamsProvider: realtime session.start callId=2d005480... caller.aadId=<redacted>
  MsteamsProvider: recording.status 2d005480... = active (realtime)
  ```
- Inline consult (`openclaw_agent_consult`). With the gate open, the caller asked a question; the model spoke the "working on it" filler and the OpenClaw agent ran and replied through the realtime model -- confirming `realtime model -> openclaw_agent_consult -> agent -> spoken reply`.
- Background task (`openclaw_agent_task`) -> Teams chat delivery. The caller said "take care of local time in the background and ping me on Teams." The model invoked the task, spoke the ack ("I'll message you on Teams"), the caller hung up, the agent ran the task in the background, and delivered the result to the caller's Teams chat:
  ```
  MsteamsProvider: realtime session.start callId=22005480... caller.aadId=<redacted>
  MsteamsProvider: recording.status 22005480... = active (realtime)        # gate open
  [trace:embedded-run] runId=voice-realtime-task:22005480...               # background agent run (post-ack)
  sent proactive message                                                    # interim "checking now..."
  sent proactive message                                                    # final result to caller's Teams chat
  MsteamsMediaStream: connection closed 22005480...
  ```
  The two messages received in the caller's Teams chat:
  > I'm checking the local machine time now and will send it here shortly.

  > Local machine time: 2026-06-04 05:28:13 +00:00.

  The agent did real work (read the actual machine clock -- `05:28:13`, matching the run time), not a canned reply.
- Pre-recording transcripts are not retained: `recordTranscript` drops turns while the gate is closed, so the consult context only ever contains recording-active text (regression test in `msteams-realtime.test.ts`).

---

## Tests and validation

**Which commands did you run?**
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  extensions/voice-call/src/providers/msteams.test.ts \
  extensions/voice-call/src/msteams-media-stream.test.ts
node openclaw.mjs config validate
pnpm build      # clean compile of the changed extension into dist
```
Result:
```
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  18 passed (18)
```
`node openclaw.mjs config validate` -> `Config valid`.

**What regression coverage was added or updated?**
- `extensions/voice-call/src/providers/msteams.test.ts`  -  provider lifecycle: session.start -> CallManager registration, transcript -> response, barge-in (`assistant.cancel`), playTts framing/pacing, hangup/teardown.
- `extensions/voice-call/src/msteams-media-stream.test.ts`  -  WebSocket server: HMAC upgrade verification (accept/reject), replay-window rejection, `session.start`/`session.end`/`audio.frame`/`ping` parsing, outbound `send`/`close`, and **inbound payload-cap rejection** (oversized frame -> close `1009`, added for GHSA-vw3h-q6xq-jjm5).

**What failed before this fix, if known?**
- `provider: "msteams"` was rejected as an unsupported voice-call provider; there was no transport for Teams audio.

**If no test was added, why not?** N/A  -  unit tests added for both the provider and the transport.

---

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** `Yes (additive, opt-in)`  -  adds a new `msteams` provider option; existing Twilio/Telnyx/Plivo/mock behavior is unchanged. For msteams specifically, `inboundPolicy` now **defaults to `"open"`** (Teams is inbound-first), so msteams calls are accepted unless the operator sets an explicit policy.

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `Yes`  -  adds `voice-call` config under `provider: "msteams"` (`msteams.{port,bindAddress,path,sharedSecret}`, plus `streaming` and `tts` blocks). `msteams`-scoped config/validation behavior in `config.ts`:
1. `msteams` is exempt from the `fromNumber` requirement (it never places outbound PSTN calls).
2. `inboundPolicy` defaults to `"open"` for `msteams` when unset.
3. **Required transport:** when `provider: "msteams"`, `validateProviderConfig` now **requires** `msteams.port` and `msteams.sharedSecret` (so a misconfigured runtime fails validation instead of starting but never binding). `msteams.path` has a schema default (`/voice/msteams/stream`).
4. **SecretRef-compatible secret:** `msteams.sharedSecret` accepts the same `SecretInput` shape as `twilio.authToken` (string or `${ENV}`/secret ref), resolved via `resolveMsteamsSharedSecret` and registered in `configContracts.secretInputs`.
No data migration.

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `Yes`  -  introduces a **WebSocket server** (operator-configured port) that accepts connections from the external worker.

**What is the highest-risk area?** The WebSocket transport (`msteams-media-stream.ts`)  -  it listens on a network port and accepts external connections carrying call audio.

**How is that risk mitigated?**
- **Loopback by default:** the WS server binds `127.0.0.1` unless the operator sets `msteams.bindAddress` to a specific trusted-network interface  -  it is never exposed on all interfaces implicitly.
- Every HTTP upgrade is authenticated with **HMAC-SHA256** over `timestamp + callId` using a shared secret, compared in constant time (`safeEqualSecret`).
- **Replay protection:** upgrades whose timestamp drifts beyond a configurable window (default 60 s) are rejected.
- **DoS bounds:** 64 KB inbound payload cap; caps on total / pending / per-IP connections.
- Inbound message bodies are validated with a strict `zod` discriminated union.
- **SecretRef secret:** the shared secret is a `SecretInput` (supports `${ENV}`/secret refs), required by config validation, and never logged.
- **Lifecycle-managed:** the server is started as an awaited step of runtime init (a failed bind aborts startup and is cleaned up, rather than being swallowed) and stopped on runtime teardown (the port is released; no orphaned listener).

---

## Current review state

**What is the next action?**
- Maintainer decision on the **product boundary** (see below) and review of the WebSocket auth/transport.

**What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?**
- Maintainer guidance on whether `msteams` ships bundled in `voice-call` or as a separate optional plugin (see Product boundary).

**Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?**
- **Bind-address control (P1):** the WS listener now binds loopback by default and exposes `msteams.bindAddress`; it no longer implicitly binds all interfaces.
- **Required transport + SecretRef secret (P1):** `provider: "msteams"` now fails validation without `msteams.port` + `msteams.sharedSecret`; the secret is `SecretInput`/`SecretRef`-compatible and registered in `configContracts.secretInputs` (so it no longer starts a runtime that cannot accept calls safely).
- **Lifecycle (P1):** the WS server start is an awaited runtime-init step (failed binds surface and are cleaned up) and is stopped on teardown (no orphaned port). Moved out of the provider constructor's fire-and-forget start.
- **Live audio round-trip (P1):** end-to-end caller->STT->agent->TTS->caller was captured on a real multi-turn Teams call (see Real behavior proof).
- **GitHub Advanced Security (GHSA-vw3h-q6xq-jjm5):** 64 KB `maxPayload` on inbound frames; regression test (oversized frame -> close `1009`).
- Internal worker/product names and phased-task references were removed from comments for upstream.

---

## Product boundary (maintainer decision)

`VISION.md` sets a high bar for bundling optional/commercial integrations, while the docs state `voice-call` owns call transport. This PR is written to be **additive and opt-in** and to keep that decision easy either way:
- It adds a 5th provider behind `provider: "msteams"`; default behavior of Twilio/Telnyx/Plivo/mock is unchanged, and nothing activates unless an operator configures it.
- It introduces **no new runtime dependencies** (reuses `ws`, `zod`, and existing plugin-sdk helpers) and **no commercial SDK**  -  Teams-specific Graph/`Skype.Bots.Media` code lives entirely in the out-of-repo Windows worker.
- msteams-specific logic is isolated (`providers/msteams.ts`, `msteams-media-stream.ts`, `msteams-tts.ts`, `msteams.runtime.ts`) with only thin, guarded hooks in shared files, so it can be lifted into a **separate optional plugin** if maintainers prefer that over bundling.

Author is happy to follow maintainer preference on bundled-vs-external packaging.

---

## Files changed (feature scope)

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `extensions/voice-call/src/providers/msteams.ts` | New `msteams` provider: explicit awaited `start()` (binds the WS server; replaces constructor fire-and-forget), inbound audio -> STT, response generation, TTS playback with paced PCM framing, barge-in, CallManager integration, `stop()` for teardown. |
| `extensions/voice-call/src/providers/msteams.test.ts` | Provider unit tests (updated to `await provider.start()`; connect over `127.0.0.1`). |
| `extensions/voice-call/src/msteams-media-stream.ts` | HMAC-authenticated WebSocket server; **binds loopback by default with optional `bindAddress`**; session.start/end/audio.frame/ping protocol; 64 KB payload cap; outbound send/close. |
| `extensions/voice-call/src/msteams-media-stream.test.ts` | Transport unit tests (connect over `127.0.0.1`). |
| `extensions/voice-call/src/msteams-tts.ts` | Teams PCM TTS helper. |
| `extensions/voice-call/src/msteams.runtime.ts` | Resolves the realtime-transcription provider + telephony TTS and injects CallManager/response runtime for msteams. |
| `extensions/voice-call/src/runtime.ts` | Resolves msteams provider with `bindAddress` + resolved `sharedSecret`; **starts the WS server as an awaited runtime-init step and registers `provider.stop()` in the resource lifecycle** (bind failures surface; port released on teardown). |
| `extensions/voice-call/src/config.ts` | `MsteamsConfigSchema` (`port`/`bindAddress`/`path`(default)/`sharedSecret` as `SecretInput`); `resolveMsteamsSharedSecret`; msteams exempt from `fromNumber`; `inboundPolicy` defaults to `"open"`; **requires `msteams.port` + `msteams.sharedSecret`**. |
| `extensions/voice-call/openclaw.plugin.json` | configSchema adds `msteams.bindAddress` and `sharedSecret` (`string`/object); registers `msteams.sharedSecret` in `configContracts.secretInputs`. |

---

<sub>🇦🇪 This contribution is from the team at the **Ports, Customs and Free Zone Corporation (PCFC)**, Dubai, United Arab Emirates — bringing Microsoft Teams voice into OpenClaw for public‑sector use.</sub>

target.title: fix(agents): filter unresolved model registry rows
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #66961.

- Filter generated `models.json` provider rows before write so model-bearing providers only survive when they have a usable execution contract: `baseUrl` plus usable auth, or an explicit runtime-accepted no-key auth mode such as `aws-sdk` / `oauth`.
- Keep resolved OAuth catalog rows only when the marker matches the provider, preserve supported local non-secret markers only on local/private endpoints, and drop stale remote/local/OAuth-looking markers.
- Improve the session/model-selection error for catalog-visible models blocked by `agents.defaults.models`.

## AI assistance disclosure

I used AI assistance for implementation, review, and proof collection. I reviewed and understand the affected path: generated `models.json` planning/merge, local auth marker resolution, model allowlist diagnostics, and the Gateway `sessions.patch` behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: unresolved generated custom providers are excluded from `models.json`, runtime-accepted OAuth/aws-sdk and supported local-marker exceptions still work, and a catalog-visible but disallowed Codex model reports the allowlist fix.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, fake local `CODEX_API_KEY`, explicit loopback Gateway port/token.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
corepack pnpm exec tsx .artifacts/issue-66961-proof/run-proof.ts
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ openclaw models list --all --provider codex --plain
codex/gpt-5.4

$ ensureOpenClawModelsJson(loadConfig({pin:false}))
{"agentDir":"$PROOF_DIR/state/agents/main/agent","wrote":true}

$ inspect generated models.json
{
  "unresolvedPresent": false,
  "oauthAuthPresent": true,
  "oauthAuthHasApiKey": false,
  "oauthLocalLookingMarkerPresent": false,
  "chutesApiKey": "oauth:chutes",
  "amazonBedrockPresent": false,
  "amazonBedrockHasApiKey": false,
  "responsesLocalPreserved": true,
  "ollamaDockerPreserved": true,
  "ollamaSidecarPreserved": true,
  "lmstudioLocalPreserved": true,
  "singleLabelCustomLocalMarkerPresent": false,
  "remoteLocalMarkerPresent": false,
  "codexModels": [
    "gpt-5.4"
  ]
}

$ openclaw gateway run --bind loopback --auth token --allow-unconfigured
gateway_ready: true

$ backend callGateway sessions.patch {key, model}
{
  "ok": false,
  "name": "GatewayClientRequestError",
  "message": "model not allowed: codex/gpt-5.4 (model is listed in the catalog, but not allowed by agents.defaults.models; add \"codex/gpt-5.4\" or \"codex/*\" to agents.defaults.models)",
  "gatewayCode": "INVALID_REQUEST"
}
```

Observed result after fix: unresolved token and non-env SecretRef providers are not written, missing-key `oauth` and blank `aws-sdk` auth stay keyless under the current runtime contract, matching `oauth:chutes` is preserved, local `custom-local`/`ollama-local`/`lmstudio-local` rows are preserved only on supported local endpoints, Ollama sidecar service-alias URLs survive refresh, unsafe single-label/remote local-marker rows are dropped, and `sessions.patch` reports the concrete allowlist change needed for `codex/gpt-5.4`.

What was not tested: real provider calls, real OAuth refresh, real AWS SDK credential resolution, and remote provider network requests. The proof uses fake local credentials and exercises admission/model-selection behavior only.

## Root Cause

Generated and merged `models.json` rows could keep model-bearing providers that were not executable by the runtime registry. A non-empty `apiKey` field was treated too broadly, so unresolved token auth, non-env SecretRef placeholders, stale OAuth markers, blank keys, and local no-auth markers attached to remote endpoints could survive into generated provider config.

The model registry requires model-bearing providers to have `baseUrl` and usable auth, except for explicit missing-key modes like `aws-sdk`. When invalid generated rows survived, they could poison catalog loading or produce confusing model-selection failures.

## Dependency contract

No external dependency contract changed.

Internal contracts checked:

- `src/agents/sessions/model-registry.ts` validates model-bearing provider rows with `baseUrl` and `apiKey`/OAuth, and currently accepts missing-key `aws-sdk` and `oauth` auth modes.
- `src/agents/models-config.providers.secrets.ts` can emit matching OAuth markers such as `oauth:chutes` after OAuth-backed provider auth is resolved.
- `extensions/ollama/src/discovery-shared.ts` treats `ollama-local` as the documented synthetic local key for local Ollama endpoints.
- `extensions/lmstudio/openclaw.plugin.json` declares `lmstudio-local` as a bundled non-secret local marker.

## Regression Test Plan

- Generated write path drops unresolved token, stale OAuth-looking, non-env SecretRef, unsafe local-marker, and remote-local-marker providers.
- Generated write path preserves matching OAuth marker, missing-key `oauth`, `aws-sdk`, and supported local marker providers.
- Merge path drops stale invalid existing provider rows and preserves only self-contained existing rows.
- Runtime auth resolution normalizes supported local markers and rejects remote/non-local marker use.
- Gateway/session and cron model-selection tests cover the improved catalog-visible allowlist message.

## Security Impact

Positive: unresolved or stale credential markers are no longer persisted as executable model provider rows.

The local marker rule is intentionally conservative. It accepts known non-secret local markers only on loopback/private/local endpoints, IPv6 local ranges, known host-backed local aliases, or the documented Ollama service alias. It rejects OAuth marker syntax and arbitrary single-label OpenAI-compatible hosts.

## Human Verification

I reviewed the generated models path, auth marker predicate, model registry admission contract, model-selection diagnostics, focused tests, autoreview findings, `codex review` findings, and isolated Gateway proof output.

## CODEOWNERS / owner review

Touched auth files match `.github/CODEOWNERS` entries for `@openclaw/openclaw-secops`:

- `/src/agents/*auth*.ts`
- `/src/agents/**/*auth*.ts`

Expected owner review: `@openclaw/openclaw-secops`.

## Changelog

No `CHANGELOG.md` edit. This is a contributor fix; release-note context is in this PR body.

## Review conversations

- Autoreview found an OAuth-marker over-admission issue for markers ending in `-local`; fixed by excluding colon marker syntaxes from local marker classification.
- Autoreview found arbitrary single-label host over-admission; fixed by narrowing the exception to the documented `ollama` service alias.
- ClawSweeper found a compatibility issue for existing missing-key `auth: "oauth"` rows; fixed by preserving the current runtime-accepted OAuth contract while still dropping stale OAuth marker strings.
- `codex review --disable image_generation --base upstream/main` found that matching OAuth markers must be preserved; fixed by accepting only `oauth:<providerId>` for the same provider.
- `codex review --disable image_generation --base upstream/main` found that existing local markers could override refreshed real local provider keys; fixed by letting the refreshed key win.
- Autoreview found that Ollama service-alias base URLs could be lost during refresh; fixed by using the same alias-aware local marker contract for base URL preservation.
- Final autoreview: accepted findings fixed. A repeated unused-import finding for `isLocalBaseUrl` was rejected after source search and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/model-auth.ts` proved the import is used.
- Final `codex review --disable image_generation --base upstream/main`: clean, no actionable correctness issues.

## Risks

- The local-marker predicate still needs maintainer judgment for future plugin-owned non-secret markers. The current rule is conservative and driven by bundled local provider contracts.

## Behavior tradeoffs

- Matching `oauth:<providerId>` is treated as usable generated auth evidence for that provider; missing-key `auth: "oauth"` rows are preserved to match the current runtime contract; stale or mismatched OAuth marker strings are dropped.
- Arbitrary single-label OpenAI-compatible hosts are not treated as local no-auth endpoints. Users with non-standard hostnames can provide real credentials or use a supported local/private endpoint form.

## Scope boundaries

- Does not change `agents.defaults.models` allowlist semantics.
- Does not add runtime compatibility for non-env SecretRefs in generated `models.json`.
- Does not change the current missing-key OAuth runtime contract.
- Does not change provider discovery, provider calls, OAuth refresh, AWS SDK auth, or plugin registry behavior.
- Does not edit docs or changelog.

## Validation

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/models-config.merge.test.ts src/agents/models-config.skips-writing-models-json-no-env-token.test.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.normalize-keys.test.ts src/agents/models-config.runtime-source-snapshot.test.ts src/agents/model-selection-resolve.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.model.test.ts src/agents/models-config.applies-config-env-vars.test.ts src/agents/models-config.uses-first-github-copilot-profile-env-tokens.test.ts
corepack pnpm tsgo:core && corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test
corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/model-auth-local.ts src/agents/model-auth.ts src/agents/model-auth.test.ts src/agents/model-selection-shared.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/agents/model-selection.ts src/agents/models-config.merge.test.ts src/agents/models-config.merge.ts src/agents/models-config.plan.ts src/agents/models-config.skips-writing-models-json-no-env-token.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/model-selection.ts src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts

target.title: feat(channels): add pre-auth access requests and grouped DM allowlists
target.body: ## Summary This PR adds a silent access-request workflow for blocked Telegram and WhatsApp direct messages. - Emit observation-only pre-auth hooks before pairing challenges or rejection. - Support grouped DM allowlists and propagate trusted `sender_group` metadata. - Extend `/allowlist add dm --group` to add senders or move them between groups. - Auto-load trusted bundled or managed `access-request` hooks while keeping mutable workspace hooks explicitly opt-in. - Document a workspace hook example and clean-install troubleshooting requirements. This matters because unknown senders currently have no silent path to request access without entering an agent session or receiving an automatic reply. The intended outcome is a bounded, privacy-conscious pre-auth observation surface that trusted hooks can use for operator-side approval workflows. Out of scope: automatically approving senders, running the model for blocked messages, or auto-enabling mutable workspace hook code. Success means blocked senders remain blocked until manually approved, while a configured phrase can notify the operator without creating a session or reply path. Reviewers should focus on the pre-auth trust boundary, workspace hook opt-in behavior, allowlist normalization, and grouped-entry config handling. ## Linked context Which issue does this close? N/A - no linked issue. Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? N/A. Was this requested by a maintainer or owner? No known maintainer or owner request. This is an external contribution based on a clean-install access-request workflow. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Unknown Telegram and WhatsApp DM senders can silently request manual access without creating an agent session, running the model, or receiving a reply. - Real environment tested: Clean Docker Compose OpenClaw installations configured with WhatsApp and Telegram `dmPolicy: "allowlist"` and an explicitly enabled workspace `access-request` hook. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Configure the workspace `access-request` example with environment variables. 2. Run `openclaw config set tools.profile messaging`. 3. Run `openclaw config set commands.config true`. 4. Recreate the container with `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`. 5. Send the configured access phrase from a blocked WhatsApp number. 6. Approve the sender from the operator chat with `/allowlist add dm --channel whatsapp --group restricted `. - Evidence after fix: 1. WhatsApp blocked sender receives no reply after sending the access phrase. 2. Operator receives the silent WhatsApp access request notification. 3. WhatsApp sender admitted via `/allowlist add dm --channel whatsapp`. 4. WhatsApp admitted sender receives a normal agent response. 5. Telegram blocked sender receives no reply after sending the access phrase. 6. Operator receives the silent Telegram access request notification. 7. Telegram sender admitted via `/allowlist add dm --channel telegram`. - Observed result after fix: Unknown senders on both WhatsApp and Telegram sent the configured access phrase and received no reply. The operator received a silent notification with ready-to-use /allowlist approval commands tailored to each channel. After approval, the sender was admitted to the restricted group. The access phrase used in this test was a custom phrase configured via OPENCLAW_ACCESS_REQUEST_PHRASE. The workspace example ships with Let me in as the default value; operators are encouraged to change it before deploying. - What was not tested: Multi-account deployments were not tested live. - Proof limitations or environment constraints: Phone numbers, gateway tokens, and private endpoints must remain redacted. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this PR, blocked senders were rejected before any trusted pre-auth observation hook existed. ## Tests and validation Commands run: ```bash node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs README.md node --import tsx scripts/generate-bundled-channel-config-metadata.ts --check pnpm build git diff --check ``` Focused Vitest validation covered 18 files and 383 tests across Telegram, WhatsApp, hook loading, hook mapping, config reload, inbound metadata, and grouped allowlist edits. Focused Vitest command: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/channel.allowlist.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/dm-access.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/inbound-context.contract.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/config-accessors.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/access-control.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/process-message.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-allowlist.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/allowlist-config-edit.test.ts src/hooks/internal-hooks.test.ts src/hooks/loader.test.ts src/hooks/message-hook-mappers.test.ts src/plugins/wired-hooks-message.test.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts src/infra/heartbeat-runner.returns-default-unset.test.ts ``` WhatsApp E2E validation: ```bash node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts ``` Regression coverage added or updated: - Telegram grouped schema, allowlist editing, blocked-DM pre-auth emission, and sender-group inbound context. - WhatsApp grouped schema, config accessors, pre-auth access control including LID handling, monitor propagation, and E2E coverage. - Core grouped `/allowlist` mutations, internal hook events, plugin hook mapping, config reload behavior, and trusted prompt metadata. Before this fix, blocked Telegram and WhatsApp DMs had no pre-auth observation event, and DM allowlist entries could not carry access-group metadata. ## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. The config schema adds optional grouped DM `allowFrom` entries. Existing string entries remain supported. No migration is required. Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. What is the highest-risk area? The new pre-auth hook exposes blocked-sender DM content to trusted handlers before admission. How is that risk mitigated? The hook is observation-only, sessionless, model-free, reply-free, bounded, and fire-and-forget. Mutable workspace hooks remain explicitly opt-in. Trusted bundled or managed hooks may auto-load only when an account uses `dmPolicy: "allowlist"`. Sender identities are validated and untrusted notification fields are sanitized. ## Current review state What is the next action? Submit for maintainer review. What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? CI and maintainer review pending. Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? Earlier review findings were addressed: - Restricted automatic loading to trusted bundled or managed hooks; workspace hooks remain opt-in. - Preserved WhatsApp grouped entries through monitor config snapshots. - Regenerated Telegram bundled config metadata for grouped `allowFrom` entries. ## Agent Transcripts Two redacted English reviewer artifacts are available: - [Curated implementation transcript summary](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28520597/openclaw-pre-auth-agent-transcript.en.md) — recommended for a quick review. - [Full translated implementation transcript](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28520607/openclaw-pre-auth-agent-transcript.full.en.md) — preserves the approved transcript ordering and original truncation boundary for detailed audit context.

target.title: fix(telegram): answer callback query before sequentialize to prevent timeout
target.body:
fix(telegram): answer callback query before sequentialize to prevent timeout

Telegram callback queries have a ~15s server-side timeout for `answerCallbackQuery`. When an agent turn is already running for the same chat/topic, grammY's `sequentialize` middleware queues the callback update. By the time it reaches the handler, the answer window has expired and the user sees a spinning loading indicator on the button that never clears.

The root cause is that `answerCallbackQuery` runs inside the sequentialized handler chain rather than being answered immediately when the update arrives. Answering the callback is idempotent and has no ordering dependency on the agent turn.

## Changes
- `bot-core.ts`: Add a `bot.use` middleware before `sequentialize` that immediately calls `answerCallbackQuery` for any update containing `callbackQuery`
- `bot-handlers.runtime.ts`: Keep existing `answerCallbackQuery` call as fallback (idempotent, safe to call twice)

## Real Behavior Proof

### Problem

Before this fix, when an agent turn is running and the user presses an inline button:

1. The `callback_query` update enters `sequentialize` middleware
2. The sequential lane is busy (agent turn is running)
3. The update is queued behind the agent turn
4. By the time the callback reaches the handler, Telegram's 15s timeout has expired
5. User sees a spinning button that never clears

### Fix

After this fix, the `bot.use` middleware before `sequentialize` immediately answers the callback:

```typescript
bot.use(async (ctx, next) => {
  if (ctx.callbackQuery) {
    await answerCallbackQuery().catch(() => {});
  }
  return next();
});
```

This runs before `sequentialize`, so the callback is answered immediately regardless of whether the agent turn is busy.

### Evidence

Unit tests verify the middleware order:
- `it("answers callback query before sequentialize blocks")` — verifies `answerCallbackQuery` is called when the early middleware runs, before sequentialize

### Testing
- TypeScript compilation passes (`tsc --noEmit`)
- The change is purely middleware order adjustment; existing handler behavior is unchanged
- `answerCallbackQuery` is idempotent — calling it twice is safe

Fixes #42156

target.title: fix(vertex): support eu and us multi-region endpoints
target.body:
fix(vertex): support eu and us multi-region endpoints

## Summary
**Problem:** Vertex AI multi-region endpoints (`eu`, `us`, `global`) use the global host `aiplatform.googleapis.com` without a location prefix. Only `global` was handled; `eu` and `us` were incorrectly prefixed (e.g. `eu-aiplatform.googleapis.com`), causing 404 errors from Google.

**Fix:** Add `eu` and `us` to the multi-region check alongside `global` in both the Google transport stream and Anthropic Vertex provider catalog.

**Out of scope:** No changes to regional endpoint handling (europe-west1, us-central1, etc.) or Vertex AI model configuration.

## Linked context
Closes #89891

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Setting `GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION=eu` causes every Vertex AI call to fail with 404 because the host is constructed as `eu-aiplatform.googleapis.com` instead of `aiplatform.googleapis.com`
- Real environment tested: Windows 10, Node.js v22.11.0, OpenClaw latest main (commit 70a989a, 2026-06-03)
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Added regression tests for `eu` and `us` multi-region endpoints and ran them via vitest
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output showing the tests passing:
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts -t "eu multi-region|us multi-region"

 ✓  google  extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts
   ✓ uses global host for eu multi-region (regression #89891)
   ✓ uses global host for us multi-region (regression #89891)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  2 passed (2)
```
- Observed result after fix: `eu` and `us` now resolve to `https://aiplatform.googleapis.com` (correct), while regional endpoints still use the location-prefixed host. The URL includes `/locations/eu/` and `/locations/us/` in the path, confirming the multi-region location is preserved in the API path while the host is global.
- What was not tested: Live Vertex AI call with `eu` endpoint (requires Google Cloud credentials)

## Tests and validation
- `extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts`: 2 new regression tests for `eu` and `us` multi-region endpoints
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist
- userVisible: Yes (Vertex AI eu/us endpoints now work instead of returning 404)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Only adds two more values to an existing multi-region check; regional endpoints unchanged

## Current review state
- Addressed ClawSweeper P1: Added focused tests for eu, us, and global multi-region endpoints
- [x] AI-assisted (built with Claude Code)

target.title: feat(whatsapp): add readOnly account mode
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- WhatsApp accounts had no way to operate in a purely passive / monitoring mode — any triggered session may send replies to the chat, with no deterministic mechanism to suppress all outbound writes.

Why does this matter now?

- Enables non-intrusive monitoring, archival, and ambient-awareness use cases (e.g. connecting an account to observe a group without the risk of the agent responding). 

What is the intended outcome?

Setting `readOnly: true` on an account puts it into passive observation mode. OpenClaw receives
and processes inbound messages normally but never writes anything back to WhatsApp on that account:

- **Outbound messages are always suppressed** — no message, poll, or reaction will ever be sent,
  regardless of trigger (agent reply, agent initiative, or explicit owner request).
- **Typing indicators are suppressed** — neither the connect-time presence announcement nor
  per-turn composing presence is sent.
- **Owner slash commands are silently dropped** — commands like `/reset` are interactive and
  confirm via a reply; on a read-only account that reply can never be sent, so the command is
  dropped before processing.
- **Read receipts are independent** — governed by `sendReadReceipts` separately from `readOnly`.

Typical use: a personal number used to read and summarize conversations without ever replying on
your behalf; or a compliance / archive account that must not send under any circumstances.

Feature documentation was added to the WhatsApp channel docs.

Developed with the help of github co-pilot using Claude Sonnet 4.6

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No runtime toggle — changing `readOnly` requires a gateway restart (same lifecycle as all account-level config).
- No new agent tool to set `readOnly`. By design, `readOnly` is not in the `assertGatewayConfigMutationAllowed` allowlist, so agents cannot mutate it.
- No UI surface — config only.

What does success look like?

- Agent sessions run normally (`prompt.submitted` → `model.completed`), tools are called, model generates replies — but nothing reaches the WhatsApp socket when `readOnly: true`. Gateway log shows `Dropped X (read-only)` lines at `info`, no `Sent X` lines. Removing the flag restores normal behavior with `Sent X` confirmations.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Guard coverage across all outbound paths: `send-api.ts` (`sendMessage`, `sendPoll`, `sendReaction`), `monitor.ts` (connect presence, pre-dispatch gate), `deliver-reply.ts` (auto-reply path), `send.ts` (`sendMessageWhatsApp`, `sendReactionWhatsApp`, `sendPollWhatsApp`).
- The `dropReason` propagation chain added to `WhatsAppReplyDeliveryResult` in `deliver-reply.ts`, and whether the unified `normalizeWhatsAppSendResultDropped` helper in `send-result.ts` is the right abstraction boundary.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #78963 (partial — this PR covers the passive / silent-account part of the request).
Other requests in that issue not involving the LLM can be addressed in separate PRs.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Owner implementation.


## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** WhatsApp account silences all outbound sends when `readOnly: true`; gateway log confirms drops at `info` with `dropReason: "read-only"`; normal sends still confirmed when `readOnly: false`.
- **Real environment tested:**
  - macOS
  - Model: `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Toggled `readOnly` in `openclaw.json`, restarted gateway, triggered agent turns from WhatsApp group or direct channel.
- **Evidence after fix:** screenshot comparisons and gateway log excerpts — see Screen Capture and Gateway log sections below
- **Observed result after fix:** All live test areas passed.
- **What was not tested:** Poll sending (`sendPollWhatsApp`) — no live test; covered by code inspection and same guard/log pattern as reactions. The implicit auto-reply pipeline (`reply()` / `sendMedia()` closures in `inbound/monitor.ts`) was not live-tested — the model always uses the explicit `message` tool; covered by unit tests in `deliver-reply.test.ts` and `monitor-inbox.read-only.test-support.ts`.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** `readOnly: true` composing suppression has no app-level log; behavioral confirmation only (user observed no typing indicator on device.

Configuration used in the tests:
```json
  "channels": {
    "whatsapp": {
      "enabled": true,
      "accounts": {
        "test": {
          "readOnly": false,  // toggeled
          "groupPolicy": "allowlist",
          "groupAllowFrom": [
            "*"
          ],
          "groups": {
            "120363406415684625@g.us": {
              "requireMention": false,  // modified
              "systemPrompt": "Be nice!"
            }
          },
          "debounceMs": 0
        }
      }
    }
  }
```
**Manual test summary**

| #   | Area                            | Passes run                         | Outcome                                                                     |
| --- | ------------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 1   | Text reply                      | `readOnly=false` · `readOnly=true` | ✅ Both passes                                                               |
| 2   | Media reply                     | `readOnly=false` · `readOnly=true` | ✅ Both passes                                                               |
| 3   | Composing indicator             | `readOnly=false` · `readOnly=true` | ✅ Both passes (behavioral; no app-level log)                                |
| 4   | Reaction sending                | `readOnly=false` · `readOnly=true` | ✅ Both passes                                                               |
| 5   | Control command drop            | `readOnly=false` · `readOnly=true` | ✅ Both passes                                                               |
| 6   | DM access-control pairing reply | `readOnly=true`                    | ✅ Pairing reply suppressed — `sendTrackedMessage` catch-all guard exercised |

---

### Execution & Integration Logs

Captured 2026-06-03, account `whatsapp-account-test`, model `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`,
gateway restarted between `readOnly=false` and `readOnly=true` passes for every test.

---

#### Test 1 · Text reply

**Purpose:** Verify that the primary `sendMessageWhatsApp` send path in `send.ts` drops the outbound message and logs `Dropped message (read-only)` when `readOnly=true`, while model execution and read receipts are unaffected.
**Expected `readOnly=false`:** `Sent message <id>` log line with a real WA message ID; round-trip latency in the hundreds of ms (socket write to server).
**Expected `readOnly=true`:** `Dropped message (read-only)` log line; no `Sent message` line; no WA message ID; latency in single-digit ms (local return only).

**Model response:** _"It's currently sunny and 27°C (about 81°F) outside! ☀️"_

#### Test 3 · Composing indicator

**Purpose:** Verify that both outbound presence writes are suppressed when `readOnly=true`: (a) the connect-time `sendPresence("available")` call in `monitor.ts`, and (b) the per-turn `sendPresenceUpdate("composing", chatJid)` call during agent execution. Neither should reach the Baileys socket.
**Expected `readOnly=false`:** `Sent global 'available' presence on connect` in the log; typing indicator (`…`) visible to the sender on device during the run.
**Expected `readOnly=true`:** No `Sent global 'available' presence on connect` line; no composing indicator visible on device. (`sendPresenceUpdate` has no app-level log; suppression is confirmed behaviorally.)

**User message:** _"please let me know what is the most important news today, and summarize it for such that it will include only one paragraph"_

#### Test 4 · Reaction sending

**Purpose:** Verify that `sendReactionWhatsApp` in `send.ts` logs `Dropped reaction (read-only)` and does not write to the Baileys socket when `readOnly=true`. Also validates the logging fix applied in this PR — the function must respect the `WhatsAppSendResult` returned by the guard rather than unconditionally logging `Sent reaction`.
**Expected `readOnly=false`:** `Sent reaction "👍"` log line; reaction emoji appears on device.
**Expected `readOnly=true`:** `Dropped reaction (read-only) "👍"` log line; no reaction on device; no `Sent reaction` line.

#### Test 5 · Control command (`/reset`)

**Purpose:** Verify that owner slash commands (e.g. `/reset`) are silently dropped before entering `onMessage()` / `process-message.ts` when `readOnly=true`. These commands confirm via a WhatsApp reply; since that reply can never be sent, the command must not run at all.
**Expected `readOnly=false`:** `inbound web message` log line; command processed; `Sent message` confirmation of the `✅ Session reset…` reply.
**Expected `readOnly=true`:** No `inbound web message`; no `session turn created`; no `Sending message` / `Sent message`. Gateway goes idle. Drop is silent (pre-processing gate, not a post-processing `Dropped` log).

`readOnly=false` — 13:42:08–13:42:14 IDT:
```

13:42:14+03:00 info  [whatsapp]           Skipping recent outbound WhatsApp echo 3EB0637332FB17595E29F3 for 120363406415684625@g.us
```
→ `inbound web message` present — command entered normal processing path. `Sent message` confirmed;
`✅ Session reset…` reply delivered. No LLM turn involved (`/reset` is a direct command turn).

`readOnly=true` — 13:44:01 IDT (gateway restarted at 13:43:33):
```
13:44:01+03:00 info  [web-inbound]        inbound message

```
→ NO `inbound web message` — guard fired before `onMessage()` / `process-message.ts`.
NO `session turn created`. NO `Sending message` / `Sent message`. System idle after.
Drop is silent (no "Dropped" log) — this is a pre-processing gate, not a post-processing drop.

#### Test 6 · DM access-control pairing reply — `readOnly=true`

**Purpose:** Verify the `sendTrackedMessage` catch-all guard in `monitor.ts` (the sole guard on the DM access-control path, which bypasses `send-api.ts` entirely). When an unauthorized DM triggers an "Access not configured" pairing reply, the reply must be suppressed when `readOnly=true`.
**Expected `readOnly=true`:** `whatsapp pairing request` logged (access control evaluated the sender); no `Sending message` / `Sent message` for that sender; pairing reply not received on device.

An unauthorized DM arrived while `readOnly=true` was active. `sendTrackedMessage` catch-all
guard at `monitor.ts:432` is the sole guard on this path (it bypasses `send-api.ts` entirely).

```
17:51:48+03:00 verbose [whatsapp/access-control]  whatsapp pairing request sender=… name=…
17:51:48+03:00 debug  [whatsapp/inbound]           Skipping recent outbound WhatsApp echo 3EB06E56A3AF4F292C9F1E for …@lid
```
→ `whatsapp pairing request` logged — access control ran and evaluated the sender.
NO `Sending message` / `Sent message` for this sender anywhere in the log.
"Access not configured" pairing reply was NOT received on device — `sendTrackedMessage` guard suppressed the Baileys `sock.sendMessage()` call.
(The echo log entry carries an inbound-echo ID from an earlier session, not a new outbound send.)

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```
pnpm test extensions/whatsapp                                                    # 860 tests, 82 files — all passing
pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts
pnpm check
pnpm check:test-types                      
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `whatsapp-web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts` — new E2E test covering `readOnly` drop logging for the auto-reply path.
- `deliver-reply.test.ts` — new unit tests for `dropReason` propagation in `WhatsAppReplyDeliveryResult`.
- `monitor-inbox.read-only.test-support.ts` — shared test-support fixture for `readOnly` inbound scenarios.
- `send.test.ts` — new boundary-level `it.each` test asserting that a policy drop from `sendMessageWhatsApp` and `sendPollWhatsApp` returns `messageId: ""`, not the internal `"dropped"` sentinel.

What failed before this fix, if known?

N/A — new feature, not a bugfix PR.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A — tests were added.


## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes — new behavior when `readOnly: true` is set: the account goes silent. No change for existing accounts (flag is optional, absence is equivalent to `false` / current behavior).

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes — new optional `readOnly` boolean field on WhatsApp account config in `openclaw.json`. Defaults to `false` (existing behavior preserved). No migration needed; no existing config files are affected.

commands.review_summary:
This PR changes the shared identity-derived mention regex builder to use explicit CJK-aware boundaries for Han-character names and adds regression tests for single- and two-character CJK names.
